section.title: Deliver Me from My Enemies
section.sectionRationale: A Davidic lament composed when Saul sent armed men to watch David's house by night to kill him at daybreak, 1 Samuel 19:11-17, this psalm turns mortal danger into a pattern of praise, the enemies who prowl like snarling dogs are met by a psalmist resolved to sing of God's strength by morning.
section.thesis: Though enemies encircle his own house by night, David's urgent plea for deliverance turns, before the psalm ends, into resolved praise, for the God who is his fortress will expose the proud in His own time, a pattern fulfilled in Christ, who was likewise watched by hostile men yet prayed mercy over His enemies rather than their ruin.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the urgent plea for deliverance, verses 1-5, closing with Selah; the prowling enemies described as snarling dogs, contrasted with the psalmist's watchful trust, verses 6-10; the prayer that God expose and judge rather than instantly destroy, verses 11-13, closing with Selah; and the refrain of the prowling dogs answered at last by a resolved song of praise, verses 14-17.

verses.text: Deliver me from my enemies, O my God; protect me from those who rise against me.
Deliver me from workers of iniquity, and save me from men of bloodshed.
See how they lie in wait for me. Fierce men conspire against me for no transgression or sin of my own, O Lord.
For no fault of my own, they move swiftly to attack me. Arise to help me, and take notice.
O Lord God of Hosts, the God of Israel, rouse Yourself to punish all the nations; show no mercy to the wicked traitors. Selah

units.label: They return in the evening, snarling like dogs
units.note: the prowling enemies met by watchful trust

verses.text: They return in the evening, snarling like dogs and prowling around the city.
See what they spew from their mouths, sharp words from their lips: "For who can hear us?"
But You, O Lord, laugh at them; You scoff at all the nations.
I will keep watch for You, O my strength, because You, O God, are my {{misgab|fortress}}.
My God of {{chesed|loving devotion}} will come to meet me; God will let me stare down my foes.

units.label: Consume them in wrath
units.note: a prayer for exposure and judgment, not silent vengeance

verses.text: Do not kill them, or my people will forget. Scatter them by Your power, and bring them down, O Lord, our shield.
By the sins of their mouths and the words of their lips, let them be trapped in their pride, in the curses and lies they utter.
Consume them in wrath; consume them till they are no more, so it may be known to the ends of the earth that God rules over Jacob. Selah

units.label: But I will sing of Your strength
units.note: the refrain answered by resolved praise

verses.text: They return in the evening, snarling like dogs and prowling around the city.
They scavenge for food, and growl if they are not satisfied.
But I will sing of Your strength and proclaim Your loving devotion in the morning. For You are my fortress, my refuge in times of trouble.
To You, O my strength, I sing praises, for You, O God, are my fortress, my God of loving devotion.

misgab.gloss: A high, secure stronghold set safely above the reach of the pursuer. David names God, not any physical refuge, his misgab three times in this psalm, framing the whole prayer as confidence in God's own unassailable protection rather than escape by his own cunning.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion. Used three times in this psalm's closing movement, verses 10, 16, 17, it is the ground of David's confidence that the God who seems distant in the crisis will come to meet him and prove faithful.

basic.context: David wrote this psalm when Saul sent men to watch his house by night, meaning to kill him at daybreak, 1 Samuel 19:11-17. His wife Michal helped him escape through a window. The psalm moves from an urgent cry for rescue through a picture of enemies prowling like snarling dogs, to a resolve to sing of God's strength once morning comes.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm really was prayed on a specific, terrifying night, and it teaches that crisis prayer can end in praise before the danger itself has passed. David asks God not simply to kill his enemies but to expose them publicly, so that his people would remember and learn.
basic.originalLanguages: David calls God his misgab, fortress, a high place safe from the pursuer, and celebrates God's chesed, His steadfast, covenant loving devotion, in the very same breath as his fear. The one who feels hunted still knows himself loved.
basic.typology: The armed men who watch David's house to kill him foreshadow the hostile authorities who watched Jesus to find a charge against Him. And David's prayer that God expose rather than instantly destroy his enemies anticipates God's patient justice, fulfilled and transformed at the cross.
basic.christ: Jesus, like David, was watched by hostile men seeking His life, Mark 3:2. But where David prays God would scatter and expose his enemies, Jesus goes further, absorbing the assault Himself and praying, 'Father, forgive them,' Luke 23:34, turning the psalm's cry for judgment into the gospel's offer of mercy.
basic.god: God is refuge and fortress for the hunted, sovereign judge of the nations who governs even surrounded by danger, and one whose settled, public justice, not frantic self-defense, will in the end expose every scheme of the wicked.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 19:11-17 supplies the historical crisis behind the psalm. Mark 3:2 shows Jesus similarly watched by hostile men. Luke 23:34 shows Jesus praying for the forgiveness of His executioners rather than their destruction.
basic.oneStory: David's night of hunted danger and his resolve to sing of God's strength by morning previews the pattern fulfilled in Christ, hunted by His enemies yet trusting His Father as fortress, whose mercy toward His killers opens the way for every enemy of God to become, through faith, one of His own.

context.literary: A personal lament framed by refrain and Selah, moving from urgent plea, verses 1-5, through the repeated image of prowling, snarling dogs, verses 6-10 and 14-15, to a prayer for the enemies' public exposure, verses 11-13, and closing in resolved praise, verses 16-17.
context.historical: Composed, per its superscription, when Saul sent men to watch David's house at night to kill him in the morning, 1 Samuel 19:11-17, David's wife Michal helped him escape through a window, and he fled to Samuel at Ramah.
context.canonical: One of several Miktam psalms of David tied to specific moments of danger, alongside Psalms 56 and 60, it models turning a very concrete, personal threat into a pattern of trust that outlives the crisis itself.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice, in the face of a specific mortal threat, both an urgent plea for rescue and a settled resolve to trust God as fortress, modeling how crisis prayer can end in praise before the danger itself has passed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's particular night of danger and his prayer for God's justice on his specific enemies; it prescribes as an abiding pattern turning to God as refuge in danger and trusting His justice rather than taking vengeance into one's own hands.

debates.question: Does verse 11, 'Do not kill them, or my people will forget,' teach that prolonged exposure of the wicked is preferable to their swift end?

views.label: The prayer reflects a specific pastoral and didactic concern, that Israel witness and remember God's justice being carried out, not merely hear that it happened.
views.case: The clause 'or my people will forget' ties the request directly to corporate memory and instruction, a concern fitting for God's anointed king responsible for his people's ongoing faith.

views.label: The verse expresses confidence that God's justice will be public and undeniable rather than hidden, so that visible vindication, not the prolongation of suffering, is the point.
views.case: The parallel prayer in verse 13, 'so it may be known to the ends of the earth that God rules over Jacob,' shows the goal is God's public vindication before all nations.

debates.bearing: Either way, the prayer is not personal vindictiveness but a plea that God's justice be seen and known, a pattern the New Testament affirms in its own confidence that God's judgment, though sometimes delayed, will finally and visibly vindicate His people, 2 Peter 3:9-10.

study.originalLanguages: David names God his misgab, fortress, three times, framing his confidence not in escape by cunning but in God's own unassailable protection. And he twice celebrates God's chesed, His steadfast, covenant loving devotion, the ground of hope that the God who seems distant will come to meet him.

typology.type: Armed men sent to watch David's house and kill him, superscription and verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Hostile authorities similarly watched Jesus to find a charge against Him, Mark 3:2, Luke 6:7, the righteous one hunted by the powerful.

typology.type: The prayer that God expose and scatter the enemies rather than instantly destroy them, so His people 'do not forget,' verse 11
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in God's patient justice that delays final judgment for the sake of witness and repentance, 2 Peter 3:9, and in Christ, who bore the assault of His enemies and prayed for their forgiveness rather than their ruin, Luke 23:34.

study.christ: David's plea to be delivered from murderous men who watch his house anticipates the greater Son of David, Jesus, whom hostile authorities also watched to find a charge against Him, Mark 3:2. Yet where David prays God would scatter and expose his enemies, Jesus goes further, absorbing the assault Himself and praying, 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do,' Luke 23:34, transforming the psalm's cry for judgment into the gospel's offer of mercy, while still affirming that God will one day judge every unrepentant enemy of His people.
study.god: God is refuge and fortress for the hunted, sovereign judge of the nations who governs even when His people are surrounded, and one whose settled justice, not their frantic self-defense, will in the end expose every scheme of the wicked.

crossReferences.note: The historical setting behind the psalm's superscription, Saul sends men to watch David's house to kill him, and Michal helps him escape through a window.
Religious leaders watch Jesus to find a charge against Him, echoing the hostile surveillance David describes.
Jesus prays for the forgiveness of His executioners, transforming David's plea for judgment into the pattern of the cross.

study.oneStory: David's night of hunted danger and his resolve to sing of God's strength by morning previews the pattern fulfilled in Christ, hunted by His enemies yet trusting His Father as fortress, whose mercy toward His killers opens the way for every enemy of God to become, through faith, one of His own.

quiz.q: What historical situation prompted this psalm, per its superscription?

options.text: When Saul sent men to watch David's house to kill him.
options.note: The superscription ties the psalm directly to 1 Samuel 19:11-17, when Saul's men surrounded David's house by night.

options.text: When the Philistines seized David in Gath.
options.note: That crisis lies behind Psalm 56, a different danger David faced.

options.text: When David fled from his son Absalom's rebellion.
options.note: That crisis lies behind Psalm 3, a later and different danger in David's life.

options.text: When David was crowned king over all Israel.
options.note: This was a moment of triumph, not the mortal danger this psalm describes.

quiz.q: What repeated image describes the enemies in verses 6 and 14?

options.text: Snarling like dogs, prowling around the city.
options.note: This refrain frames the psalm's central movements, picturing relentless, threatening hostility.

options.text: Roaring like lions in the wilderness.
options.note: A similar predator image, but not the one this psalm actually uses.

options.text: Circling like vultures overhead.
options.note: The psalm's image is dogs prowling the city, not birds circling.

options.text: Standing silently at the gates.
options.note: The enemies are described as loud and prowling, not silent.

quiz.q: What does David ask God to do with his enemies in verse 11, rather than killing them at once?

options.text: Scatter them by Your power and bring them down, so that his people do not forget.
options.note: David wants God's justice publicly seen and remembered, not simply completed out of sight.

options.text: Convert them into allies overnight.
options.note: The psalm prays for judgment on the wicked, not reconciliation with them.

options.text: Kill them immediately and completely.
options.note: Verse 11 specifically asks God not to do this yet, so his people will remember.

options.text: Ignore them entirely and let them go free.
options.note: David is not indifferent to justice; he actively prays for God's judgment on them.

quiz.q: How does the psalm end, in verses 16-17?

options.text: With a resolved song of praise: 'I will sing of Your strength... for You are my fortress.'
options.note: The psalm moves from urgent fear to settled praise before the crisis has necessarily ended.

options.text: With a description of the enemies' final destruction.
options.note: Judgment is prayed for earlier, verses 11-13, but the psalm's actual close is praise, not a scene of destruction.

options.text: With a demand that God act immediately or David will give up.
options.note: The tone of the ending is settled trust and praise, not an ultimatum.

options.text: With continued despair and no resolution.
options.note: The psalm resolves into confident praise, not unresolved despair.

quiz.q: How does Jesus' response to His enemies deepen this psalm's prayer for justice?

options.text: Jesus prayed for the forgiveness of His executioners, offering mercy while still affirming God's coming judgment on the unrepentant.
options.note: Luke 23:34 shows Jesus extending mercy even as the New Testament affirms final judgment remains God's to give.

options.text: Jesus asked God to destroy His enemies immediately, exactly as David prayed.
options.note: Jesus instead prayed for their forgiveness, going beyond David's specific request.

options.text: Jesus fought back physically against those who arrested Him.
options.note: Jesus explicitly refused violent resistance, Matthew 26:52-53.

options.text: Jesus ignored His enemies completely and never addressed them.
options.note: Jesus directly addressed His executioners in prayer, Luke 23:34.

questions: David was watched by armed men in his own home yet resolved to sing of God's strength 'in the morning.' What situation are you facing right now where you need to trust God's protection through the night before you can sing in the morning?

The refrain 'they return in the evening, snarling like dogs' repeats in verses 6 and 14. What relentless pressure or fear keeps circling back in your life, and how does David turning that repetition into praise, rather than despair, speak to you?

David prays that God would expose and scatter his enemies rather than destroy them outright, 'or my people will forget,' verse 11. Where do you need patience for God's justice to be seen rather than demanding it happen instantly?

Jesus, watched by hostile men just as David was, prayed for His enemies rather than for their destruction. How does knowing Christ absorbed judgment for His enemies change how you pray for people who oppose you?
David calls God his fortress and His loving devotion in the same breath, verses 16-17. Where do you need both God's strength and His steadfast love this week?

translationNotes.note: Deliver renders a Hebrew verb for high, safe rescue; the same root idea reappears in the fortress imagery of verses 9, 16, and 17, unifying the psalm's plea and its praise.
Snarling like dogs pictures the roaming, scavenging street dogs of the ancient Near East, an image of restless, threatening hostility repeated at verse 14 to frame the psalm's movements.
Loving devotion renders chesed, God's covenant loyalty; David's confidence rests not in his own strength but in this steadfast character of God.

The historical superscription is not rendered as verse text, consistent with the established convention in this corpus (see Psalm 3, Psalm 18, Psalm 56), and is instead described in context.historical. No em or en dashes present.
